data.title: Frontier Airlines Remains A 'Hold' As Strategy Shift Has Yet To Crystalize
data.description:
Frontier Group Holdings stock (ULCC) has climbed 26.2% since my last report, outperforming the S&P 500’s gain. The stock price now slightly exceeds the $5.25 price target I set, providing a good moment to look at what lies ahead for Frontier Airlines and update my price target.

Wall Street Expectations For Frontier Airlines In Q4 2025

Analysts are expecting Frontier Airlines to report revenues between $946.5 million and $988.9 million, with a midpoint estimate of $971.4 million, indicating 3.1% lower sales compared to a year ago. We do note that the company does not have a strong record of beating expectations, as it has missed estimates three quarters consecutively, and over the past 12 quarters, it beat estimates only three times.

Earnings per share estimates range between $0.10 and $0.18, with a midpoint estimate of $0.14, indicating a 39% decline year-on-year. Luckily, on earnings per share, the track record is substantially better with two misses over the past 12 quarters.

In recent months, revenue estimates for the fourth quarter have barely changed, with a 0.6% downward revision over the past month and a 2.75% downward revision over the past three months. Five analysts revised estimates down, while three analysts revised estimates upward. EPS estimates, however, substantially improved with a 48% improvement over the past three months and a 66.4% improvement over the past month. Revisions of analysts were split equally.

Strategic Shifts Drive Revisions In Favor Of Frontier Airlines

The upward revisions for Frontier Airlines are likely driven by changes that the low-cost carrier is making as well as changes in the competitive landscape. Spirit Airlines has exited 36 overlapping routes and reduced capacity by 30% on another 41 routes, driving a 2% competitive capacity reduction in the fourth quarter with a potentially even bigger impact on competitive capacity decline into 2026. Furthermore, the company is seeing more loyalty revenues, and in the spring of 2026, the company is launching first class. Low-cost carriers have seen substantial pressure on operations as inflation and pilot shortages inflated costs, and that led to Spirit Airlines substantially downsizing and other low-cost airlines rethinking their cabin segmentation. That should drive performance in the fourth quarter and into 2026, with full maturity for first-class introduction expected in roughly three years.

ULCC Stock Remains A 'Hold'

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Frontier Airlines is a bit of a complex company to value since the company has no credible median EV/EBITDA, given that it became a public company during the pandemic. The current EV/BITDA of 8.6x shows that at current prices the stock is fully valued for the expected earnings in 2025. For 2026 and beyond, I am applying a 6.6x EV/EBITDA, which is one unit lower than the 7.6x EV/EBITDA for profitable peers. I am applying that discount given that at present it is uncertain how the company will finance new aircraft acquisitions, including pre-delivery payments. My assumption currently is that the company will engage in sale-and-lease-back transactions combined with adding debt. As long as the company does not have a positive operating cash flow, I do believe it is justified to apply a discount and maintain a hold rating even though potential upside is 46%. The upside, in my view, is too fragile and uncertain given the company’s high capital needs, and I also believe that the company has to rethink its contracted fleet additions. In some ways, Frontier is facing a dilemma that many airlines face, where they are burning cash and adding new airplanes to amortize costs and turn positive on operating cash flow.

If we look at the financial performance, the expectation is that competitive capacity declines and a reshaping strategy for Frontier Airlines, including a first-class product and growth in loyalty revenues, will result in a substantial EBITDA margin recovery. Operating cash performance, however, is expected to only be positive by 2027, with current fleet additions being financed through sale-and-lease-back transactions as well as debt. For ultra-low-cost carriers, I do believe net-debt leverage should be in the 3x-5x range. Frontier Airlines is expected to achieve that in 2026, but a lot of the appeal for the company is going to hinge on its ability to actually turn a positive operating cash flow.

Conclusion: Frontier Airlines Fleet Additions Provide Pressure

The fleet planning of Frontier Airlines is a bit of a double-edged sword. On one hand, the company seems to require additional scale to drive down unit costs and achieve a positive operating cash flow. On the other hand, that requires substantial capital needs that currently can only be covered through net debt additions and engaging in sale-and-lease-back transactions. So, higher stock prices are possible, but I maintain a "Hold" rating as I believe we first need to see substantial signs that Frontier’s strategy is working.

data.title: Firefly Aerospace: Buy This Speculative Bet Focused On Execution
data.description:
Thesis

I view Firefly Aerospace Inc. (FLY) as a speculative Buy.

Below I will analyze the company with my main focus on its operational progress and its ability to execute missions consistently in space.

The value comes from building a real business around the technology that can generate regular revenue.

The stock recently entered Russell 2000 Index (RTY). This improved its visibility and helped, through the increased liquidity the stabilization of the shareholder base.

Improving the pace of missions and strengthening the presence in government and defense programs can change the image of the company.

This investment is for those who are attracted by volatility and are willing to wait for operational maturity.

Company Overview

Firefly is a company focused on launching payloads into space.

These payloads are typically small to medium sized and include satellites, scientific equipment or missions for government agencies.

The Rocket "ALPHA" is one of the basic tools of the company and is designed for a specific launch and not for very heavy payloads.

This means that Firefly is not trying to compete the biggest players of the sector but wants to serve a specific part of the market.

This choice reduces the need for very large investments and allows for a more focused commercial strategy while the Blue Ghost program brings Firefly into missions related to the Moon.

This sector is gradually gaining importance, as government organizations invest in permanent presence and infrastructure.

IPO and Market Sentiment

Firefly went public at a time when investors were showing great interest in companies operating in space.

There was a lot of excitement around the so called space economy and many investors believed that companies in the sector could grow quickly.

This resulted in high initial valuations ($45 price) and very optimistic expectations.

In practice running a space company proved to be more difficult and slower than the market had expected.

Firefly dealt with technical problems and delays in its missions which consequently affected its image while the pace of execution was not consistent. This led to disappointment and to a sharp drop in the stock after the listing.

This development shows the difference between vision and reality.

It is a common phenomenon in new technology companies where plans take time to be properly implemented.

In the case of Firefly, the company did not change course or abandon its investments to calm the market.

Instead, it continued to invest in facilities, personnel and process improvements.

This as i see it, is a positive sign, because it shows that management is focused on building the right foundation and not just supporting the stock in the short term.

News Flow and Strategic Direction

Recently, Firefly’s management has shown that it has shifted priorities and is paying more attention to how the company actually works.

Its focus is now on proper organization, better quality control and improving daily operations and this is important as mistakes in space sector are costly. For me, the key issue is getting the Alpha rocket back on a stable mission schedule because one successful launch is not enough.

That is valuable is that the company can perform missions over and over again, without major delays.

The inclusion of the stock in the Russell 2000 index helps Firefly become better known to institutional investors and increases the liquidity of the stock.

This doesn’t change the company’s fundamentals but it reduces volatility and make the stock more stable.

At the same time, Firefly’s increasing involvement in defense-related projects makes practical sense.

Defense contracts tend to last longer and offer better revenue visibility.

Financials

Q32025 results show that Firefly can generate revenue when missions are active and run without delays.

Revenue increased by 38% vs last year, which in my view, show that customers want the company's services.

This is easier to understand when you look at the business model. Launches are not evenly distributed throughout the year and revenue tends to be concentrated in specific quarters.

For this reason, the strong quarterly volatility does not particularly concern me as I consider this normal for a space company that is not yet operating at a steady, repeatable pace.

Losses remain high and the company continues to burn capital, which is entirely expected for a space technology company in its growth stage. I do not evaluate Firefly based on EPS or short-term profitability. The key metric i watch is liquidity and the time it gives management to improve operational execution.

The company almost $1 billion in cash, which gives it a strong safety buffer. This gives the company the time to fix issues, stabilize operations and turn growing demand into more predictable income.

This point is very critical to the whole investment evaluation as it distinguishes Firefly from smaller firms with less financial flexibility.

Valuation

The stock trades at $30 per share.

Company reports negative earnings and high cash consumption. This means that traditional metrics like P/E are not useful shifting the spotlight to enterprise value to revenue and operational development scenarios.

The company has strong liquidity with close to $1 billion in cash. This minimizes the funding risk and gives the company time to improve execution. Its backlog of $1.3 billion gives visibility into future demand only if missions are delivered as planned.

Now let's analyze three scenarios.

In the bear case scenario, execution is unstable, delays continue and trust is not rebuilt. The market is likely to keep a high risk premium. In this scenario a share price near $12 is expected (60% below current levels) as there will be loss of confidence in the company’s ability to mature operationally.

In the base case scenario where launches become more stable but not fully repeatable, a price of $30 to $35 or flat to about a 17% increase, seems reasonable.

In the bull case scenario, with stable and repeatable launches, stronger defense exposure and clearer revenue trends, the stock could be rerated toward $80 (>165% higher than today) as Firefly is seen as a real operating business rather than just a technology bet.

Firefly is a high risk company but with very high potential reward.

For this reason, the stock is a speculative buy suitable only for investors who can handle volatility.

On Nasdaq, FLY shows its aggressive nature. In less than 5 months of trading since its listing (in August 2025), it has already suffered a massive 62% loss and an extraordinary recovery of +79%.

Technically, the range of $31.90 to $32.50 is the main resistance after the big gap from the 21% drop on September 30.

The buy signal from $19 is still valid and the stock’s inclusion in the Russell may be helping to keep buying interest alive.

Risks

As i see it, the most significant risk is the technical reliability of the missions, as success in the space industry requires a high degree of precision and zero tolerance for errors.

A single failed mission can damage the company’s reputation, delay its launch schedule and weaken trust among existing and potential customers.

Another risk is the ability to keep a steady launch schedule. If you ask me irregular execution creates revenue volatility, reduces predictability and makes long-term planning harder for both management and investors.

Firefly is still using cash, which is normal but it must keep enough liquidity.

Another weakness is that the company also depends on a limited number of programs and customers. This makes it more exposed to cancellations, delays or budget cuts (especially in government contracts).

Because the valuation already assumes future success, the stock can be extremely volatile to negative news.

Competition in the space industry is intense. There are new and existing competitors that want the market shares with lower cost.

Conclusion

In short, Firefly Aerospace is a high-risk investment that needs strong and steady execution in a tough industry.

The addition of the stock to Russell 2000 increases visibility and liquidity. This could reduce extreme volatility in the long term.

This does not change the fundamentals but it supports the investment case. The stock is perfect for investors who love risk and are attracted by high volatile stocks.

Firefly is a speculative Buy based on execution progress and not short-term results.

data.title: Quince Therapeutics: Good Potential In Orphan Disease In Q1 With Platform Potential
data.description:
Thesis

Quince Therapeutics (QNCX) is doing a Phase 3 trial in Ataxia-Telangiectasia [A-T], an orphan disease without known treatment affecting 10,000 patients worldwide. Its Phase 3 trial called NEAT addresses issues related to failure in the past, and historical causes for failures in similar diseases. I discuss these below.

If successful, it may become the first approved treatment for A-T worldwide, and the company’s therapy could be applied in other diseases as well.

The Phase 3 readout is therefore probably around the corner. Last patient last visit took place in December 2025 and results are due mid-Q1 2026, which I assume could be next month.

The A-T market is over $1 billion, which may lead to significant upside in case of success, irrespective of eDSP’s further platform play potential.

Quince Therapeutics

eDSP in Ataxia-Telangiectasia: earlier results

Quince Therapeutics’ treatment candidate eDSP for A-T adds dexamethasone to a patient’s red blood cells, allowing for slow release of that drug contrary to traditional toxic administration of corticosteroids.

Quince acquired eDSP from Erydel some years ago, after Erydel had failed a 6-month Phase 3 trial A-T called ATTeST, which was high-profile and the largest clinical study ever run in A-T. The primary reason that was quoted for the failure of that trial was lack of symptom progression in patients 10 years and above. Lack of symptom progression is a typical cause for trial failures in ataxias.

A statistically significant result was achieved in the 6-9 year age group receiving the high dose, showing a 4.8-point difference on the ICARS rating scale (p=0.0016).

Placebo patients aged 6-9 progressed much faster than older patients, with a 4.3 point difference between both groups.

That result aligns with positive results from an earlier 6-month Phase 2 trial in A-T in 21 patients, which also showed a 4-point difference on ICARS, with a 12.75 point improvement in less neurologically impaired patients.

In the extension study of that trial, ICARS scores for patients on drug further improved while patients no longer on drug worsened or remained stable.

NEAT trial

Quince is currently finalizing its randomized placebo-controlled NEAT trial in 105 patients, for which enrollment started in 2024 and was complete in July 2025. The last patient completed his last visit on December 15, 2025. Results are awaited mid-Q1 2026, which should in principle be next month.

NEAT is essentially a better-designed ATTeST trial in order to achieve a statistically significant results in the primary analysis population, i.e. the 6-9 year old age group. I think chances are very high that positive results could be achieved. The trial at least seems to be a clear improvement compared to the ATTeST trial, which as such did not achieve a bad result, just one that was not statistically significant – yet superior to placebo – in the entire patient population.

Targeted patient population with highest disease progression

The 6-9 year old age group is the primary analysis population for this trial. Studies, both internally as externally, show that symptom progression is highest in the 6-9 year-old age group, and then considerably slows down.

This could explain the earlier ATTeST trial failure, and is a considerable improvement to trial design in my eyes.

Further trial improvements

Historical failures in ataxia, generally orphan diseases, are, among others, related to variability of symptom progression across patients, rare disease recruitment challenges and endpoint insensitivity. With the trial focusing on 6-9 year old patients and having been fully enrolled, the first two potential issues seem to be tackled.

Additionally, the NEAT trial uses RmICARS, as requested by the FDA under a Special Protocol Assessment, as its primary endpoint. RmICARS focuses on domains that were more sensitive to change in the ATTeST trial.

Changes on ICARS, mICARS and RmICARS between the ages 6 and 10 from the ATTeST trial reveal scoring differences of respectively +19%, +34% and +46%, indicating highest sensitivity for RmICARs in the 6-9 year age group which forms the primary analysis population of the NEAT trial. During its Investor Day, Quince stated that the RmICARS is probably the most sensitive endpoint, and I see this confirmed by a patient-reported comparison of different scales indicating the RmICARS is equally good or better at tracking differences in disease progression.

The NEAT trial incorporates further trial modifications which I interpret as positive.

  • The high dose, which had better results in the ATTeST trial, is the only one used in the NEAT trial.
  • Trial powering is different; NEAT is 90% powered vs. 80% usually to detect a 2.4 difference on RmICARS, with a 3-point confidence interval for patients on treatment, and 3.7 points for placebo, powering the study for a delay in progression of disease of about 24%.
  • The NEAT trial has a 4% discontinuation rate, vs. 24% for the ATTeST trial, which could be related to the lack of Covid-related issues;
  • NEAT only has 4% missing data vs. 26% for ATTeST, which is good as NEAT’s powering assumptions were based on the ATTeST’s trial’s missing data.
  • Rollover rates in the extension study, which were already high in the ATTeST trial [96%], are now 99%.

One further difference to be noted is that the extension trial data collected from the ATTeST trial, including a good safety profile, is now available. That OLE ran from 2018 to 2022, allowing Quince to collect data of about 70 patients that were on therapy for over three years. The high rollover and adherence rate in both trials indicates that the therapy may be having an effect on patients.

Looking at those differences, I think NEAT may have successfully tackled the obstacles that impeded ATTeST’s success.

One could argue that the NEAT trial does not have biomarkers progression as endpoint, and in that sense, the trial could still have been better designed. For example, in a Phase 2a/b trial testing triheptanoin in A-T, NfL was used as a biomarker, yielding good results. In light of the SPA which should allow the FDA to approve eDSP on the basis of one pivotal trial, I do not see that point of particular concern.

Estimating chances of success

The ATTeST trial included 176 patients in the intent-to-treat population. Randomization across high-dose, low-dose and placebo was spread evenly across the entire mITT population. A total of 33 patients aged 6-9 were on the high dose.

The mITT 6-9 year old age group had a 4.8 difference in scoring on the mICARS rating scale, with a p-value of 0.0016, which means high statistical significance.

The difference between ICARS and RmICARS is not only the scoring (a total of 100 points versus a 29-point total), but also where the scoring most relates to. Quince presented a poster in that respect, showing among others:

  • That progression from age 6-10 was +19% on ICARS, +34% on mICARS and +46% on RmICARS;
  • The ICARS subcomponents which increased more than 25% in that age group were walking [100%], standing capacity [40%] and sitting [27%], which relate to posture and gait;
  • That RmICARS captures most progression in that age group – note that total points on RmICARS are less than one third of total scores on ICARS, meaning that an average of ~18 points relates to about 60%of the entire scoring on that scale, whereas an average of about ~44 relates to less than half of total scoring on ICARS;

No age-related trends were seen on speech, oculomotor and kinetic function.

The RmICARS rating scale focuses exactly on those areas, with most of the points attributed to posture and gait disturbance, and much less to kinetic function, and without any rating for oculomotor disorders.

Focusing on the areas where progression is highest should positively influence Cohen’s D/effect size.

Based on the ATTeST trial, if one were to estimate significance on RmICARS for the 6-9 year old age group, statistical significance would be met with a p-value of 0.009, which is again highly statistically significant (the cut-off is p < 0.05).

In summary, the RmICARS rating scale tracks disease progression focused on 6-9 year old patients. When, in 33 patients, the RmICARS rating scale was used, this led to a p-value of 0.009.

In comparison, the current NEAT study’s primary analysis population, i.e. the 6-9 year-old patients, comprises 83 patients which, assuming half are on placebo, is 26% more than the same group of patients in the ATTeST trial. Assuming 4% missing data would bring that population down to about 80. NEAT is powered at 90% to detect a 2.4 point difference, but its powering assumptions for missing data were based on ATTeST’s 26% whereas NEAT only has 4% missing data. Furthermore, NEAT trial has a 4% discontinuation rate, vs. 24% for the ATTeST trial.

A larger N should directly affect the standard error and chances of statistical precision. The effective sample size is much higher, which I believe may affect statistical precision. Missing data influences variances and weakens effective sample size. In ATTeST, with 32 patients on the high dose and 24% missing data, effective sample size was probably closer to 24 patients. In NEAT, taking into account 4% missing data, we are probably looking at about a 40-patient effective sample size on therapy (and about 80 patients in the primary analysis population). In that sense, the difference of 4% [NEAT] versus 24% [ATTeST] may have a significant influence. Combining both, I think the likelihood of a false-negative result has been reduced by 66%.

Standard error should be lower than in the ATTeST trial, as it is inversely proportional to the square root of the sample size. Statistical precision should increase by 29%, and variance should decrease by 40%.

All of these also influence the minimum scoring effect needed for statistical significance. As mentioned above, NEAT was powered for a 2.4-point scoring difference on RmICARS. My estimate is that a 1.45-point difference on RmICARS would be the floor for statistical significance, allowing NEAT to substantially underperform results shown in ATTeST. That assumes a target difference of 2.4 points, a p-value 0.05, 90% statistical power, and Z-scores of respectively 1.96 (Alpha) and 1.282 (Beta).

From that perspective, my assumption would be that NEAT, in practice, is a highly powered trial to confirm an effect that was already previously seen to be highly statistically significant.

Safety

I do not see any concerns from a safety and tolerability perspective. eDSP works by taking the patient’s own red blood cells, enlarging them externally in a table-top machine to allow them to be perfused with dexamethasone, a prodrug corticosteroid, and reinserting them into the patient. Across dose groups, the only adverse events in the ATTeST trial were mild to moderate and transient, and did not need medical intervention to resolve [page 9].

Like in other indications, corticosteroid use is generally not advised in A-T as it leads to immunodeficiency issues and bone loss. Although its efficacy has been established, traditional administration leads to a temporary peak dose accompanied by toxicity, and the effect of such peak dose quickly tapers. eDSP tackles this issue because its mechanism of action allows for slow release during the course of a month, without toxicity. In that respect, Quince reported that growth and bone mineral density were not negatively affected over 24-month treatment with EryDex.

Efficacy in smaller earlier trials

Quince wishes to expand eDSP to other indications in case of success of the NEAT trial.

Earlier trials showed that this is realistic. Trials with lower doses than the dose currently used in the NEAT trial have been run in ulcerative colitis, Crohn’s disease, cystic fibrosis and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, showing reduction or termination of corticosteroid use.

Quince plans to rely on this data using the US 505(b)(2) regulatory pathway for approval of eDSP in A-T.

Valuation potential

Quince plans to file an NDA with the FDA in 2H 2026 in case of good results, and has a strategic partnership with Option Care Health, America’s leading outpatient infusion provider, to that purpose.

The FDA granted eDSP fast-track designation, and both the FDA and the EU’s EMA have granted it orphan drug designation, which should allow for respectively seven and ten years of exclusivity as of the moment of approval in case of approval in the US and the EU.

Rare disease therapies often cost $350,000 - $1million per year. Recently approved drugs for Niemann’s Pick disease could serve as an example. Annual costs of both treatments Miplyffa and Aqneursa are respectively $503,600 - $1,328,000 and $366,350 - $732,650. Many of these drugs are forecast to reach $1 billion or more in peak sales. A good example is Skyclaris, acquired in 2023 by Biogen for $7.3 billion, for a similar-sized patient population, with estimated peak sales of $1 - $1.5 billion. Another is Krystal Biotech’s Vyjuvek, expected to have more than $1 billion in peak sales in a smaller indication than Ataxia-Telangiectasia. KRYS currently has a +$7.3 billion market cap.

From that perspective, I believe there may be substantial room for upside in case of success for A-T alone. The development pipeline for A-T is also rather minimal. Intrabio is furthest advanced, with the drug Aqneursa or levacetylleucine which has been approved for Nieman’s Pick Disease in September 2024, where it competes with Zevra Therapeutics’ (ZVRA) Mypliffa, which had been approved as the first treatment for that indication a week earlier. Aqneursa operates through activation of cerebral glucose metabolism in the cerebellum. From the perspective of its MoA, there does not seem to be a similarity to eDSP. Results from an earlier phase 2 trial failed to reveal significant benefits. Initial results from Intrabio’s Phase 2 trial were not retrieved, but the extension phase of that trial apparently yielded positive results.

Quince mentioned during their recent Investor Day [at 1h.19min.] that, should a competing asset advance to approval, eDSP has the ability to be dosed concomitantly.

eDSP’s platform play potential should furthermore not be underestimated. eDSP may additionally find different applications in several indications, for which the use of corticosteroids is still the standard of care. The first indication the company is looking at is Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD), for which treatment with corticosteroids provides delayed disease progression, but comes with similar toxicity issues. The recently approved Duvyzat is indicated for patients who are already on corticosteroids, after having established a benefit in such patients in a placebo-controlled trial. Capricor Therapeutics recently reported success in DMD as well, but from an allogeneic cell therapy, and its results have been criticized. Quince plans to start dosing its first patients in a Phase 2 study in Duchenne’s in the course of this year.

Finances

Quince had $26.3 million in cash at the end of Q3 2025. In June 2025, Quince completed a financing comprising $11.5 million in upfront proceeds and up to $10.4 million in warrants at premiums to the market price at the time.

Net loss for the Q3 2025 quarter was $13.3 million. Over the first nine months of 2025, net cash used in operating activities was $30.9 million, i.e. $10.3 million on average.

Quince expects cash to be sufficient until into the second quarter of 2026, i.e. after the Phase 3 readout for A-T, and intends to raise more in case of success to fund further activities, including NDA filing and commercialization. Upon a positive readout, such a further financing is common.

Risks

Several risks to a Buy thesis exist here.

Quince’s stock has increased significantly over the past months. The market may correct at least some of that in the near term, but looking at the chart, I believe that correction may already be in place.

Another risk is that placebo may outperform previous historical results.

There is a chance that the Phase 3 NEAT trial that Quince is running does not generate statistically significant results, or that the market does not interpret the results as a success. One could argue that the same mechanism of action has failed to generate statistically significant results in this indication before—primarily due to age-related study design issues of ATTeST discussed earlier.

Even in case of success, the regulatory authorities would still have to approve the drug, and commercialization would still have to be successful afterwards. There is always also a competitive threat, as mentioned above.

Conclusion

Quince Therapeutics is slated to release topline data from the NEAT trial in Ataxia-Telangiectasia in mid-Q1 2026, which I assume will be next month.

I believe several elements speak in favor of potential success here.

First, the safety data seems excellent. There is apparent efficacy of corticosteroids in Ataxia-Telangiectasia, but that efficacy comes with downsides in the case of conventional administration. eDSP seems to solve that, as has been shown in an open-label Phase 2 trial and later Phase 3 ATTeST trial.

The NEAT trial setup deals with apparent trial flaws from the past, such as the inclusion of patients with plateaued progression in the primary analysis population, and Covid-related data quality issues. My analysis of the chances of success as set out above indicates a high probability for the trial to be successful.

There seems to be alignment and support from the regulatory authorities, as shown by the SPA on the Phase 3 trial, fast track designation, and orphan drug designations.

Given the promising setup and high-risk, high-reward prospects, I think a Buy rating is in place here.

data.title: Thryv Holdings: Ignore Near-Term Lumps And Buy For Deep Value
data.description:
If there was ever a perfect time for active portfolio management, now it is. A growing number of market participants are sounding off alarms on valuations amid uncertain macro footing and rising geopolitical tensions. To me, the best answer is to make a significant shift in portfolio allocations toward small- and mid-cap value names, many of which are rebound plays.

Thryv Holdings, Inc. (THRY) is a perfect example of this. The company is transitioning from being a Yellow Pages publisher to a small business software provider, and we continue to find evidence of momentum in the company’s software efforts, despite sharply negative market sentiment that has taken down Thryv’s stock by more than 60% over the past year.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Thryv in October, when the stock was hovering around $12 per share. I’ll fully acknowledge that my buy call on Thryv was ill-timed and that this has been a position that has required tremendous patience. Irrespective of the past, when I assess Thryv with fresh eyes now, I don’t see any jarring red flags that fully merit the sharp drop in this stock, and I still like the investment for its contrarian value qualities. I’m reiterating my "Buy" rating on Thryv.

Outside deep value, here is my full long-term bull case for the company:

  • Transitioning into recurring SaaS. In the past, Thryv published physical and web Yellow Pages (which it’s now housing under its legacy marketing services segment), which had limited monetization and didn’t provide recurring revenue. Today, the company is transitioning to a more predictable, higher-margin SaaS business.
  • Growing portfolio of SaaS solutions. Thryv’s capabilities now cover CRM, payroll automation, tax compliance, social media and SEO/lead management, and marketing campaign tools, among others. SaaS is growing at a >30% pace, highlighting the effectiveness of Thryv’s transition (14% organic).
  • Intentionally targeted. Thryv is specifically segmented toward the lower end of the SMB space. It notes that CRM rival HubSpot (HUBS) typically targets customers with ~$10k or more spending, while Thryv’s sweet spot is between $4k to $8k in spend per client. The number of Thryv customers using multiple services (and thus increasing spending) is growing.
  • Already profitable. Despite its relatively small scale, Thryv already generates a near-20% adjusted EBITDA margin in its SaaS business, which few higher-growth rivals can boast.

Of course, the single biggest advantage in this stock is valuation. At current share prices near $5, Thryv trades at a market cap of $240.5 million. Netting off the $11.6 million of cash and $268.1 million of debt on the company’s latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $497.0 million.

For the remainder of FY25, the company has slightly adjusted its guidance for the year downward. It’s now calling for SaaS revenue of $460-$463 million, or 34%-35% y/y growth, versus a previously wider range of $460-$465 million. The company is, however, expecting a slightly richer adjusted EBITDA margin in this segment at $73-$75 million, or a 16% margin at the midpoint. Meanwhile, the company has held its legacy marketing services revenue expectations flat, while adjusted EBITDA is adjusted downward to $76-$78 million, or a 23.8% margin. Overall midpoint adjusted EBITDA goes to $151 million for both segments, only a dash lower than $151.5 million previously, and positions Thryv’s valuation at 3.3x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA.

These are unheard-of valuation multiples in today’s expensive market. That said, don’t take a concentrated position here. My preferred portfolio for FY26 is to spray a small allocation toward a large variety of undervalued small-cap stocks, and Thryv is a good candidate for that. Buy here and hold out for a rebound.

Q3 Download

Let’s now go through Thryv’s latest Q3 results in greater detail. Though the market had a sharp reaction to Thryv’s mixed guidance update, the quarter itself was reasonably solid:

Thryv’s total revenue in Q3 grew 12% y/y to $201.6 million, beating Wall Street’s expectations of $200.9 million (+11% y/y) by a one-point margin. SaaS revenue, which is now 58% of the company’s total, grew 33% y/y to $115.9 million. This was partially inflated by Thryv’s acquisition of the CRM platform Keap, which it bought for $80 million in October of last year and will be comped next quarter. Excluding Keap, the company notes organic growth would have been 14% y/y.

We note that one of the metrics that investors balked at was the steeper-than-expected deceleration in SaaS billings to 32%, from 46% in the prior quarter, which also prompted the slight cut to full-year SaaS revenue expectations. And while average revenue per customer continues to expand, the company faced a headwind from the noise of smaller customers migrating over from legacy marketing services. Per CEO Joe Walsh’s remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

With a large established customer base now in place, our focus is on increasing spend per customer by driving adoption of more products and solutions, especially among our high-value clients and larger businesses. This approach meaningfully expands SaaS lifetime value and is a more efficient driver of profitability.

In the third quarter, overall SaaS ARPU reached $365 with Thryv at $355, up sequentially and Keap ARPU remaining strong at $437. Seasoned NRR declined to 94% this quarter, primarily reflecting noise introduced as we transition legacy digital marketing services clients on to our modern SaaS platform.

As we systematically wind down older tech platforms on our marketing services side, we are upgrading clients to our current software offerings while honoring their previously committed pricing, vastly improving the value they receive by introducing a wave of smaller accounts into our base, which temporarily impact ARPU at the time. These accounts from our Q3 2023 migration are now cycling into the seasoned NRR calculation after crossing the 12-month threshold. The performance we're seeing from this group is consistent with the minimal initial commitments and lower propensity to expand spend compared to our higher-quality software clients. This is all part of our broader business transformation.

The company notes that its trend of multi-product adoption continues at a healthy pace. 20% of the company’s customers now use two or more SaaS products, up 4 points from 16% in the year-ago Q3.

Meanwhile, the business transition is proving lucrative for profitability. Total adjusted EBITDA more than doubled y/y to $40.8 million, reflecting a 20.2% margin that improved nine points versus 10.9% in the year-ago quarter:

Risks And Key Takeaways

Of course, there’s no doubt that Thryv faces a variety of risks. First and foremost, the company is up against substantial competition. In CRM, the company is stacked up against dozens of much better branded competitors like HubSpot and Salesforce (CRM); in payments, the company competes against Bill.com (BILL) and Intuit (INTU); and in marketing automation, it's stacked up against Adobe (ADBE), which also recently purchased Semrush to strengthen its SEO chops. On top of this, Thryv is making a difficult business transition and may not retain all of its customers in the legacy marketing services segment. Adding the cherry on top of a substantial debt load, which limits Thryv’s financial flexibility, almost cutting off the possibility of making another Keap-sized acquisition in the near term.

While risks are present, my thesis is that these risks are more than priced into a stock trading at ~3x EBITDA despite vibrant mid-teens organic SaaS growth. In my view, this remains a good, cheap diversification play to bet on.

data.title: Greenbrier Beat Earnings: The Cycle Still Wins
data.description:
Thesis

While The Greenbrier Companies, Inc. (GBX) beating earnings estimates may be the biggest news for the company, it is really about whether or not the earnings beat was of high quality, or if it is simply due to tactical moves (selling assets, resilient leasing), while the core of the company, which is cyclical, continues to suffer from the current cycle.

The headline EPS number led to market reaction. However, the negative performance of the stock indicates that investors very quickly looked beyond the beat. I like to look at all aspects of a company as opposed to just EPS. There are certainly some tactical ways Greenbrier was able to produce positive results such as asset sale proceeds and resilient leasing but the fundamentals of the underlying business remain impacted by an unfavorable cycle.

Production of rolling stock is decreasing, the backlog of orders does not show any signs of growth, and there is still considerable leverage within the company. The risk here is overestimating a defensive nature of their recent performance as opposed to a true structural improvement.

This is fundamentally the reason behind my Sell rating on GBX.

Company Overview

It's not a headline that often makes headlines, so I think it's necessary to provide an overview of the company and understand who it is, how it makes money, and where it makes its money.

This analysis is about a U.S. manufacturer of railroad equipment that is one of the largest railcar manufacturers in North America. Railcars account for almost all (90%) of Greenbrier’s railcar earnings. Its customers are primarily railway companies, transportation leasing companies, and large logistics and industrial operators. Revenue is generated through design, production, and sale of railcars, as well as providing service support and maintaining railcars owned by other firms and rented to shippers.

Railcar sales account for roughly 92% of the company’s earnings. The company’s business model is based on receiving orders for railcars from customers and then producing those railcars for delivery to those customers. The process of manufacturing railcars requires significant investment in equipment and personnel and is thus affected by the overall state of the economy and the demand for railcars.

In addition to earning income from the design and manufacture of railcars, The Greenbrier Companies earns additional income from managing a fleet of around 17,000 railcars owned by the company. The company leases these railcars to shippers and provides repair and maintenance services to those railcars and to railcars owned by other companies.

Both of these business segments are very similar to each other. Each business segment has to follow the same production and distribution cycle as railcars manufactured by the company.

The majority of the company’s earnings come from the U.S. market. Two of the company’s largest customers account for around 30% of the company’s earnings. This concentration of customers means that a significant change in the purchasing behavior of either of these customers could have a major impact on the company's earnings.

Earnings Season

The Greenbrier Companies' quarterly results released in early January are a good example of why exceeding earnings expectations alone isn't enough to support the stock. The quarterly report from early January shows that while the overall performance was better than estimated by analysts (both EPS and Revenue surpassed analyst’s estimates) a more detailed analysis of the actual performance, guidance and comments made on the investor call supports the negative reaction of the equity markets and provides justification for my Sell rating of the common stock.

What worked

Leasing was the first pleasant surprise this earnings season. As a result of the company going through a very sharp slowdown in the business cycle, leasing behaved perfectly as expected and, therefore, became a source of stability. Fleet usage remained practically the same at 98% and renewal rate exceeded the expectation. So, it was a shield that helped them.

Another positive factor was liquidity, as the company generated new cash flow and has sufficient reserves to manage the current downturn without significantly straining its balance sheet.

Thirdly, Sales. Although we did not see a significant increase in orders during the quarter, they also did not experience a decline in orders. As such, the order book remains essentially flat, not increasing or decreasing.

Management indicated during the call that they saw an increase in sales activity late in the quarter and reiterated their expectation for a stronger second half of the year, particularly in regards to volume and margin. This is cycle based guidance, not a change in trend. However, absent a clear acceleration in the cycle, I find it difficult to place a lot of emphasis on it.

What Went Wrong

If the company was working in a favorable, and if the company were operating in a favorable, expanding environment with strong industry growth expected, then the market could respond positively. However, as I will now explain below, there are other factors that are negatively impacting the company.

First, the company's core business, so the manufacturing. In the last quarter, the revenue total was $706 million, which represents a decline of approximately 19% from the same quarter in prior years. The largest number is the decrease in gross margins in the manufacturing segment. Manufacturing Gross Margin dropped to 11.0% from 15.7% in the previous quarter. The gross margin was dampened mainly by lower volumes and production inefficiency caused by slow production rates. As such, the efficiency improvement initiatives, which include reducing workforce, are logical from a cost standpoint, however, they also indicate that the company is currently operating in a defensive position during the current economic cycle.

Secondly, there are issues relative to the quality of the company's earnings. The EPS of $1.14 exceeded analyst estimates and was largely impacted by the $17.7 million in capital gain recognized by selling the company's fleet assets in the quarter. So, it's something that happens today and won't be repeated in the coming quarters. It should clearly be excluded from the analysis.

The Company's management team stated on the conference call that these sales added approximately $0.30 per share to the quarterly earnings but they also stated that these are opportunistic and not be replicable each quarter. Therefore, this is one reason why I tend to "downgrade" the company's earnings results. The improved earnings do not result from a structural increase in the company's industrial profit margins, but rather from financial and asset management tactics that can only be utilized a few times.

Third, the company has to deal with the negative trends in demand. On the conference call, the company's management team repeated that their customer base remains hesitant to make large purchases due to decreasing transportation volumes, trade policy uncertainty, and the complexity of the current U.S. environment. Although the company stated that the impact of tariffs on its earnings has thus far been minimal, the company did state that the uncertainty continues to affect the timing of when the company's customers decide to make their purchasing decisions.

Valuation

The company's forecasted EPS for fiscal 2026 is expected to be around $4.17, down more than 35% the previous number (from $6.59 to $4.17).

This does not resemble a normalizing pause as would happen during a typical recession or slow-down within an industry cycle. Instead, this represents a decrease in earnings within an industry cycle that will most likely continue into future periods. In the future, estimates indicate that the FY2027 EPS will be approximately $4.48 and FY2028 EPS will be approximately $5.70, while the three-year EPS CAGR continues to be negative (-4.7%).

The market is currently pricing-in a decline in earnings, possibly through volatility. While I may be incorrect, I do not see how you can read the earnings trajectory in any other way.

Prior to establishing a target price, I want to analyze the multiple. At first glance, the implied forward P/E ratio (11.9x, FY2026 EPS of $4.20) does not appear tight. However, I want to highlight that this multiple is based on decreasing earnings, not increasing earnings. It is not defensive by design but it is subject to cyclical normalization. Additionally, the financial leverage (Net Debt 3.5x EBITDA) introduces an extra level of complexity to the picture. Not an issue today, but enough to prevent the stock from defending its multiple, should the cycle slow more than anticipated. In my view, this is still being underpriced by the market.

Honestly, my rating is due to a combination of factors related to valuation and qualitative issues. I began with the FY2026 EPS guidance provided by management: $3.75-$4.75. I used the midpoint ($4.25) as my starting point. The stock currently trades at an implied multiple of approximately 11 times EPS. Currently, volumes are declining and leverage is high. In this context, a double-digit multiple appears to me to be a "leap of faith" rather than a conservative valuation. I could be wrong, however historically, companies such as GBX have difficulty in maintaining full multiples during cycles that turn against them.

Therefore, for my valuation analysis, I applied a multiple of 9.5 times to an EPS of $4.16. This results in a price target of $39.52.

Risks

Although Greenbrier’s exposure to unfavorable cyclicality has been reduced somewhat, its risk profile is still imbalanced.

As the company produces a considerable quantity of railway rolling stock, it is significantly more exposed to a longer destock period than expected that would not only lead to a decrease in volumes and margins but also be negative on the working capital absorption.

Moreover, it is still very financially leveraged (Net Debt 3.5x EBITDA), so any drop in earnings from the levels currently anticipated for FY2026 will be magnified and there will hardly be any room for mistakes.

Conclusion

This quarter does not solve the company's real problem. I believe the cyclical engine of the business is still stagnant and only the defensive and tactical measures are supporting earnings at this point.

Operationally, I do not believe it is wise to buy this stock right now. The asymmetric nature of the potential for gains versus the potential for loss is not favorable to the investor. The downside is apparent, i.e., a negative estimate revision or an ongoing decline in the cycle could create the risk of the investors’ loss. However, the upside would exist if/when the company starts to recover in the second-half of the year and the cycle cooperates.

data.title: Gold Reserve: There Isn't A Lot Of Upside Left
data.description:
For over a decade, Gold Reserve Ltd. (Bermuda) (GDRZF), (GRZ:CA) stock has not traded on any underlying fundamentals. Rather, it has been valued as a binary event: It either will or will not be compensated for assets that were expropriated by the Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela.

Since the capture of Nicolas Maduro, Gold Reserve’s equity has more than doubled. The market is pricing in either a greater probability of recovery of funds, a greater quantum of funds that are recovered, or a combination of these two factors. Although there may be other Venezuela-connected securities that I am unaware of that have had a greater appreciation in value, Gold Reserve is far and away the biggest mover in percentage terms when compared to Venezuelan Government Bonds, Bonds issued by Citgo's owner, PDVSA, and Rusoro Mining Ltd. (RML:CA).

In my opinion, Gold Reserve and GDRZF stock are a Sell because;

  • The move to upside has been on relatively low volume.
  • There isn't, in my opinion, a lot of upside.
  • It's hard to see how the capture of Maduro has increased either the scale of a potential payout or the probability of a payout.
  • Retail investors have a structural disadvantage when it comes to obtaining information about a wider Venezuelan restructuring, of which Gold Reserve is now potentially part of.

Figure 1: Total Return—Gold Reserve vs. Rusoro, Year-to-Date 2026

I. Given the size of the movement, trading volumes have been relatively low

The TMX (i.e., the Toronto Stock Exchange) reported that the Consolidated Trading Volume for Gold Reserve across all venues on January 7, 2026, was 593,411 shares. This represented less than half a percent of the 122,714,914 shares that Gold Reserve has issued and that are outstanding. It also represented a total monetary value of circa CAD $3 million, or USD $2 million. By January 12, the consolidated Trading Volume had decreased to 287,293 shares, or 48% of what it was on the 7th.

Approximately 60 percent of Gold Reserve shares that were traded on the 7th took place on the TMX, and the comparable figure for the 12th was 68 percent.

  • The 351,000 shares traded on the TMX on January 7th compares with the 10-day Moving Average volume of 195,000 shares and the 20-day Moving Average volume of 109,000 shares. In monetary terms, this means that an extra USD $900,000 in value was traded on the 7th when compared to the 20-day moving average.
  • The TMX figures for the 12th are: January 12th—196,438 shares, 10-day Moving average—237,760 shares, 20-day Moving average—131,122 shares.

In other words, increased trade in the shares of Gold Reserve of approximately USD $1 million has been sufficient to increase its Market Cap by more than USD $250 million.

II. How much can Gold Reserve expect to receive from the proceeds of the sale of CITGO?

The following are all potential sources of funds for Gold Reserve.

1)The Special Master's Final Report In The Crystallex Case:

As of the close of January 12, 2026, Gold Reserve's market cap was USD $487.19 million, or 97.4% of the USD $500 million that it was due to receive under the Special Master's Final Report. It is worth noting that Gold Reserve's appeal of the Special Master's final decision was denied on December 19, 2026, and it is unclear if it has further legal options to pursue.

It should also be noted that Gold Reserve shareholders will not be entitled to 100% of the funds received under any settlement or award. Per Gold Reserve's 2024 Annual Report, the following will be deducted from any proceeds received.

  • "Contingent Value Rights (“CVRs”) that entitle the holders to an aggregate of 5.466% of certain proceeds from Venezuela… As of December 31, 2024, the total cumulative amount paid pursuant to the terms of the CVR from the sale of the Mining Data and collection of the Award was approximately $10 million."
  • "The bonus pool under the Bonus Plan is comprised of the gross proceeds collected or the fair value of any consideration realized, less applicable taxes multiplied by 1.28% of the first $200 million and 6.4% thereafter… As of December 31, 2024, the total cumulative amount paid pursuant to the terms of the Bonus Plan from the sale of the Mining Data and collection of the Award was approximately $4.4 million."

Table 1: Final Disposition of Proceeds of Citgo Sale per the Special Master Report

2) The Brisas Project and the Siembra Minera Project:

Questions arise from time to time about Gold Reserve’s ability to monetize its data from the “Brisas Project” and/or its ability to potentially develop this project. It would appear that Gold Reserve no longer owns the data, as its 2024 Annual Report states that,

"... we signed the Settlement Agreement whereby Venezuela agreed among other things to pay the Company a total of approximately $1.032 billion, which is comprised of $792 million to satisfy the Award (including interest) and $240 million for the purchase of our mining data related to the Brisas Project (the "Mining Data") in a series of payments that were supposed to end on or before June 15, 2019. As agreed, the first $240 million received by Gold Reserve from Venezuela has been recognized as proceeds from the sale of the Mining Data. As of the date of this MD&A, the Company had received payments of approximately $254 million pursuant to the Settlement Agreement: $240 million for the sale of the Mining Data and $14 million related to the Award.

Further,

Concurrent with the Settlement Agreement, the Company and Venezuela also agreed to pursue the joint development of a project designated as the “Siembra Minera Project” that primarily comprised the former Brisas Project and the adjacent Cristinas project. ... Siembra Minera is beneficially owned 55% by Corporacion Venezolana de Mineria, S.A., a Venezuelan government corporation, and 45% by Gold Reserve. Siembra Minera was granted by the government of Venezuela certain gold, copper, silver, and other strategic mineral rights contained within Bolivar State, comprising the Siembra Minera Project.

Venezuela had certain obligations to fund the Joint Venture, which it has yet to fulfill, and ultimately, it cancelled the Siembra mining rights. After pursuing remedies through the Venezuelan courts, Gold Reserve has since filed a claim with the ICISD.

In other words, Gold Reserve has already been paid for this data, and as things stand, the decision to develop the mine, or not, rests with the majority shareholder, Venezuela.

3) The Portuguese Case:

In February 2025, Gold Reserve prevailed in a $1.4 billion Portuguese lawsuit to recover deposits of the Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela held in Novo Banco, a Portuguese bank. As the company noted at that time,

"Recovery is not certain in the Portugal legal proceedings due to multiple factors." ... Further court orders are required to attempt to collect against any of the attached funds."

This decision is one of several commercial claims by commercial creditors, and it was later upheld by the Portuguese Supreme Court on July 9, 2025. However, the court also ruled that although it agreed that Venezuela was liable, "The Court emphasized that the issue of execution, such as whether Venezuelan assets in Portugal could be seized, was not yet before it." There is yet more litigation to come.

In any case, even if the further question regarding whether or not Gold Reserve has the legal capacity to seize these deposits, that in itself would not mean that Gold Reserve would receive compensation. In July of 2023, Novo Banco was ordered to release EUR1.3 billion of Deposits to nine separate Venezuelan state-owned entities.

So, as is the case in the U.S., there are competing claims for Venezuela's Portuguese assets. It is unclear to me how the capture of Nicolás Maduro will influence a Portuguese court to rule on these claims in favor of commercial creditors versus Venezuela itself. Further, even if the courts in Portugal do rule in favor of Gold Reserve and other commercial creditors, I again fail to see how Maduro's capture would influence them to rule that such creditors can seize the assets of a sovereign.

III. How have other markets reacted? How relevant is this to Gold Reserve?

It's estimated that Venezuela has external debts of $150 - $170 billion. Precise figures owed to commercial creditors aren't available, and the amount of money owed to Russia and China hasn't been publicized, but in any case, this represents approximately twice the amount of Venezuela’s annual GDP.

Bonds issued by Venezuela have rallied by approximately 20 percent, to a level of 40 cents on the dollar. Meanwhile, PDVSA bonds rallied by approximately 9 cents to a level of 30 cents on the dollar.

All in all, it appears that the market believes that Venezuela's ability to pay its debts has increased by more than $30 billion in the past week. Gold Reserve shareholders appear to believe that some of this added value will come to them. Let's examine this assumption more carefully.

If the remnants of the Maduro regime are ousted and a new government is installed that restructures its debt under an IMF or U.S. government-led bailout, I can see two broad outcomes.

Outcome 1: OFAC refuses to sanction the sale of Citgo from PDVSA to Amber Energy, the winning bidder in the auction of Citgo. All assets owned by Venezuela, including Citgo, are pooled with claims on future oil revenues from Venezuela. These funds and/or newly restructured bonds are then distributed to creditors according to a waterfall.

It isn't clear that ICISD creditors like Gold Reserve would have priority over bondholders, sovereign creditors like China or Russia, or other commercial claimants. Gold Reserve's claim would now be one of many, and it could very possibly be in a worse position than it is now.

Further, it's hard to see the U.S. Government sharing the proceeds of oil revenues with other parties, especially non-U.S. parties. Comments to date seem to involve giving incentives to oil companies to invest in Venezuela.

Trump said the White House would “devise a formula” to ensure that Caracas receives funds and corporations recover their investments while the US government would get any “leftover funds."

However, then there was this:

'We're not going to look at what people lost in the past because that was their fault,' Trump said before asking Lance how much ConocoPhillips lost in Venezuela. Lance said his company owed $12 billion. 'Well, good write-off,' Trump remarked.

Outcome 2: Existing settlements would be honored, and other creditors would participate in a separate restructuring. In this case, Gold Reserve would be in the same position as a week ago (eligible for funds from the Crystallex case and also potentially the case in Portugal), with the exception that there might now be a greater chance of the Siembra Minera project proceeding.

IV. Conclusion

The market values Gold Reserve by approximately $250 million more than it did at the start of the year. This valuation is supported by increased daily trade in its shares of less than one percent of its value at the start of the year.

This increased valuation is predicated on the assumption that Gold Reserve will receive extra compensation for past losses in Venezuela. However,

  • The capture of Maduro will not influence the outcome of the Crystallex case. It's already been decided.
  • The run-up in Venezuelan bonds isn't material to Amber Energy's settlement with the 2020 bondholders. It's already been agreed.
  • Gold Reserve still can't sell its Brisas Project data. It already did so.
  • It's unclear how Maduro's capture would impact the Portuguese courts one way or the other.
  • There is no way to forecast:
    • How much wealth can be created from increased oil production in Venezuela.
    • How this potential increased wealth would be distributed. Initial comments by President Trump seem unsympathetic towards compensating companies that have had losses in the past for these losses. Rather, it appears as if the U.S. Government is contemplating some sort of guarantee for future losses.
    • If Gold Reserve were eligible for a piece of this pie, what would it have to forgo in return.

Given that Gold Reserve's Market Cap is already exceeding the $500 million award mooted in the Crystallex Case, and that not all of this award belongs to its shareholders, I view the stock as a Sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Elevra Lithium: Value In Sight For NASDAQ Participants
data.description:
Uptrend Around Elevra Lithium on the NASDAQ

This article is about Elevra Lithium Limited (ELVR) on NASDAQ. If the market is signaling buying interest in the company's shares, our analysis supports the thesis that the market, in this case, is making a correct choice, as long as it weighs the positive potential for this stock.

About Elevra Lithium Limited

Who is Elevra Lithium Limited?

Below is a screenshot of the company description from Seeking Alpha.

Elevra Lithium Limited adds on its website:

Our flagship operation, North American Lithium (NAL) in Québec has positioned Elevra as a secure and reliable supplier of lithium spodumene concentrate.

North American Lithium: A Major Source for the Booming EV Market

The North American Lithium Project (hereinafter “NAL”) is considered one of the largest lithium deposits in North America. This is also confirmed by various online sources, as well as the company's website. NAL will inevitably be a crucial source for the economy committed to sustainable growth with minimal environmental impact, including through the use of lithium battery-powered electric vehicles. NAL supports the Western Hemisphere's strategy of securing its supply chains and reducing its dependence on foreign products, particularly from China, in light of geopolitical tensions. In this context, NAL enjoys an excellent reputation, reflecting the structural factor that explains the demand for Elevra lithium shares on the NASDAQ and thus justifies the buying opportunity. This holds regardless of lithium price fluctuations that could affect Elevra's growth plans. In our view, market volatility should be seen as an opportunity that occasionally contributes to building positions in Elevra.

Elevra Lithium is the sole owner of NAL. Resource information is available on Elevra’s website: 95 million tonnes of lithium at 1.15% Li₂O and an additional 49 million tonnes of lithium at 1.11% Li₂O in reserves.

Elevra Lithium Limited produces spodumene concentrate, a form of hard-rock lithium. Due to its high lithium content, this product is in high demand in the booming market for electric vehicle batteries and energy storage systems. Elevra focuses on supplying the source of lithium to these sectors.

The downturn, in which the narrative of rising investments in electric vehicles and the transition to electromobility loses momentum, leads to temporary price declines in lithium. Otherwise, the lithium price is rising due to a structural deficit in the physical market; this is the prevailing trend. A screenshot of Elevra Lithium's Annual General Meeting from November 21, 2025, confirms this. The statistics for the "Lithium Market Outlook" in the screenshot are from Canaccord Genuity Equity Research, dated November 19, 2025. We believe it is important for readers to know that the statistics do not originate from the company itself but from an external source.

Regarding the slides from Elevra Lithium Limited's presentation on Seeking Alpha (“link”), Seeking Alpha states:

The following slide deck was published by Elevra Lithium Limited in conjunction with this event.

The event in question is the 2025 Annual General Meeting, which took place on November 21, 2025.

Lithium Price Outlook

Spodumene concentrate with 6% lithium oxide (Li₂O) is a key raw material in the lithium supply chain and represents an early stage in the value chain before undergoing complex refining and purification processes to serve as a component in batteries for electric vehicles and energy storage systems. You may have encountered prices in your online research that are significantly higher than Canaccord Genuity's forecast. These substantial price differences may be related to the specific lithium product and its purity or form. The market research firm Canaccord Genuity forecasts a price of $1,500 to $1,750 per ton for spodumene concentrate with 6% lithium oxide (Li₂O) in 2026. Canaccord Genuity is not the only prominent analyst publishing assessments of supply and demand dynamics, technological advances, and market conditions. Assumptions vary largely among analysts. We would like to offer our readers another prominent opinion from Goldman Sachs. Discovery Alert cited last year's Goldman Sachs forecasts of $800 per ton in 2025, $969 per ton in 2026, $1,155 per ton in 2027, and $1,264 per ton in 2028. Discovery Alert also noted that Goldman Sachs analysts warned that “prices will remain 50% below 2023 peaks” even in the long term, due to the growing presence of Chinese lepidolite producers and the diversification taking place in the battery chemistry industry.

In summary, Canaccord Genuity predicts a bullish scenario for the spodumene price per tonne, while Goldman Sachs expects a moderate price scenario for the lithium-rich material. We need this forecast range for our analysis.

NAL Operating Results for the Financial Year 2025

First, a note to the readers: Elevra Lithium Limited was formed on August 29, 2025, through the merger of Piedmont (USA) and Sayona (Australia). Sayona had already announced its financial results for the fiscal year 2025 around August 29, 2025, the date the merger was completed. However, these figures reflect the situation of the merged company as presented at the Annual General Meeting on November 21, 2025. At this meeting, the CEO of Elevra explained the business performance following the merger. The financial presentation included key performance indicators for fiscal year 2025 and compared them with the business outlook for fiscal year 2026 under the name Elevra Lithium Limited. The business outlook for fiscal year 2026 confirms Elevra Lithium's growth trajectory in the global lithium market and continues the development already indicated in the operating and financial figures published prior to the merger. These figures now also reflect the performance of the merged company.

North American Lithium has fully met Elevra Lithium's 2025 forecasts, with increased production and sales volumes while simultaneously achieving cost savings. The investment strategies point to a solid growth trajectory. Operationally, they are also performing well. Given the expected future operational successes, we have a positive outlook for market sentiment regarding Elevra Lithium shares.

The increase in operational efficiency leads to higher production and sales volumes and lower costs. Near-full utilisation of the processing plant (93%) and the recovery of a remarkable portion of the available lithium (73%) maximise production and ensure efficient operations. The effects are clearly visible: an impressive increase to 205 kt of concentrate, or +31% year-on-year, is objectively strong growth and a clear signal that the operation is on track for the expected growth.

Production is not intended to replenish inventories in the balance sheet (US$31 million in 2025), which, assuming a price of US$850 per tonne, would represent approximately 2.5% of total spodumene concentrate production. Nor is it a sign of weaker demand in the future, as we are broadly in line with previous years and especially with 2023, when lithium prices peaked. Rather, production is aimed at meeting strong market demand, which naturally has a positive impact on sentiment around Elevra Lithium shares. The record sales volume of 209,000 tonnes would have never been achieved without the corresponding production growth. That much is certain, but, in our view, the momentum should not be seen as production driving spodumene sales volume but rather as demand for spodumene concentrate justifying the company's growth strategy: delivering record spodumene concentrate sales volume, enabled by near-full capacity utilisation and high plant efficiency. Cutbacks in government spending on electric vehicle incentive programs will impact spodumene concentrate demand in 2026, but Elevra appears to have a dedicated, albeit growing, market for the record volume of spodumene concentrate it produces, which is good, we think, for Elevra Lithium's stock price. Elevra Lithium's stock price has risen 2.5-fold since September 2, 2025, on the NASDAQ, as of this writing.

Unit operating cost sold (FOB) decreased (9% year-on-year) due to improved operating efficiency. This can, of course, be attributed to the higher concentration of lithium in the spodumene. Crucially, however, costs depend on strong demand for the product. Elevra was able to produce more spodumene concentrate, thereby spreading fixed production costs over a larger production volume and reducing production costs per unit. This is certain, but the decreasing operating costs per unit sold suggest that the market is absorbing the increased volumes, and the market is the ultimate force enabling Elevra to achieve its cost target.

Highlights for Fiscal Year 2025 with Financial Figures

Elevra Lithium Limited closed its 2025 fiscal year on June 30, 2025, and is expected to publish its next earnings report—Q2 – Q2 FY26 -- on March 11, 2026.

In fiscal year 2025, Elevra Lithium generated revenues of A$223 million and sold 209,038 dry metric tons. This resulted in a realised price of A$1,067 per ton of spodumene concentrate sold. Operating costs per ton sold (FOB) were A$1,290. Elevra Lithium closed its fiscal year 2025 with a loss after income tax of AU$381.7 million, a deterioration from the loss of AU$119 million in the fiscal year ending June 30, 2024. The worsening is attributable to higher operating costs at North American Lithium, as well as an impairment and write-down of non-financial assets (i.e., NAL's assets) of AU$271.3 million for the fiscal year ending June 30, 2025. Elevra Lithium does not yet have a positive margin, and at the close of fiscal year 2025, Elevra Lithium Limited had negative free cash flow per share of A$0.92 (about US$0.60), resulting in negative free cash flow of A$(61.8) million or US$(40.7) million (excluding debt repayment).

Elevra attempted to control the outflow of free cash flow from sustainable capital projects, not only by moderating investments in equipment and infrastructure, as leading analysts became less optimistic about the expected continued growth of the lithium price in the coming years, but also because—and this will be more to the stock market's liking—the efficiency with which processing facilities operate near full capacity requires less maintenance capital. Exploration in Quebec for new lithium resources or the expansion of existing reserves in Quebec to ensure long-term supply was financed through Flow-Through Share funding.

Also, in our view, the impact of higher costs will diminish over time as the combined company's business stabilises post-merger and the previously noted improvements in operational efficiency continue to unfold their beneficial consequences. We assume that most market participants expect this after reading the company's presentation of figures, trends, and forecasts. We also do not believe that investors are likely to be concerned about the impairment charge. This is consistent, so much is certain, but it is a thing of the past. As it was a one-time event, this spectre will no longer impact the likelihood of a positive net result. It offsets the increase in revenue from the sale of spodumene concentrate in FY25, but it will not be for the expected higher revenue in FY26.

Elevra Lithium Outlook: Production, Costs, Pricing

Spodumene concentrate production and sales from the Elevra Lithium project are expected to average 202,500 dmt in 2026, with an average cost per unit sold of A$1,225/dmt, or US$825/dmt at the time of writing.

Between Canaccord Genuity's optimistic price forecast of $1,625 per ton for spodumene concentrate and Goldman Sachs' more moderate price forecast of $969 per ton, the average price forecast for 2026 is $1,297 per ton. Assuming leading analysts are correct, and Elevra is too, with its Unit Operating Costs Sold of $825 per dmt, the expected positive shift in Elevra's profit margin could occur from 2026 onwards.

Elevra Lithium therefore intends to rebuild a more generous full-year spend for 2026 from A$20 million in 2024 to up to A$40 million per year to push the processing plant's capacity well beyond its existing capacity. That is up to 6,500/ton per day (a +44% from current levels). This is consistent with the scoping study from September 2025 regarding the expansion of the NAL operation and will also result in reduced operating costs per unit.

Q1FY26 In Line With The Growth Plan For Fiscal Year 2026

Although operations have faced some challenges, which Elevra Lithium largely anticipated in its November 2025 presentation, the first quarter of fiscal year 2026 (ending September 30, 2025) indicated, in our view, that things are on track to meet expectations for the full year. The cyclical decline in spodumene concentrate production—NAL could produce a maximum of 52,003 tons in the first quarter of fiscal year 2026—is reportedly due to lower utilisation of the processing plant and a lower lithium recovery rate. We do not see this as a cause for concern for investors. If, as it appears, the company is strategically targeting the strongest revenue period in the second half of fiscal year 2026 (January to June 2026), then the decrease in utilisation can be interpreted as a period of calm rather than an indication of structural problems in the deposits and facilities. We expect the company to benefit from increased lithium prices, as the average selling price has risen by 14% to A$1,198/tonne compared to the previous quarter, driven by higher market prices. If Elevra Lithium has focused on building up inventories for improved performance in the second half of fiscal year 2026, then in our view, even the slight increase in unit operating costs (1% compared to the previous quarter) to A$1,250/tonne is not a cause for concern. Production, driven by accelerated operations, plant utilisation, and rising sales volume, will inevitably need to be optimised. Furthermore, Elevra Lithium will be able to develop additional tons in the second half of fiscal year 2026, particularly the lithium-rich spodumene reserves that the company is currently piling up as inventory. Given the expected further strong increase in lithium prices, the company's financial position in the second half of fiscal year 2026 thus gives cause for optimistic expectations. The share price is expected to be the first to reflect the (quite positive) impact on market sentiment surrounding Elevra Lithium shares on the NASDAQ. Sales volume fell by 61% to 25,975 tons in the first quarter of fiscal year 2026. Despite the decline in sales, the NAL loss from operations of $2 million was lower than the prior quarter. This is attributable to the rise in lithium prices, but also to the fact that operating costs per unit went up only slightly despite the visible decrease in production during the quarter. And the company states:

NAL saw a net operating cash outflow of $12 million primarily due to a planned build of finished goods inventory.

This reinforces our thesis that operations are getting more and more cost-efficient, paving the way for the turning point that will ultimately allow NAL to enter a profitable era and achieve a break-even business.

Management confirmed its guidance for fiscal year 2026: Spodumene concentrate production and sales are expected to be between 195,000 and 210,000 tonnes, with a focus on the second half of the year, and unit operating costs of $1,175-1,275/dmt per ton sold.

The Potential for Further Expansion of NAL Operation

With investments of at least A$40 million per year and a good chance that CapEx can be accelerated with the tipping point toward profitability reached in 2026, driven by higher spodumene prices and lower costs of spodumene sold, the processing plant expansion project is on track. Commissioning is scheduled for the last quarter of calendar year 2029.

The projects indicate that NAL will increase spodumene concentrate production by 56%, from 195-210 ktpa (fiscal year 2026 forecast) to 315 ktpa nominal SC5.4 after the expansion is ramped up in calendar year 2030. C1 unit costs will decline by 30% to US$562/t and AISC will hover at US$680/t once the expansion is fully operational.
